sentences.text: OK.
So we're in Hamburg, in this environment-- and, of course, you know, I've got photos I'm showing you-- we had a variety, a mix of children.
They are coming from all sorts of background, and so the religious part didn't really play a-- how will I say-- a big part in it.
However--
No, I-- I felt it in me, but on the other hand, now, it was a question of survival more than which was more important.
Because, you see, at this point now, it started to come out that we were not allowed to enter into shops.
We were being discriminated against.
You know, when there was food on the table, we ate what there was.
We didn't ask.
You know, I must say this--
When the situation--
When the situation comes to that, and you're-- you're going to say, well, I'm going to be fussy.
I'm going to-- I wasn't the type to say, I'm going to rather starve to death than eat non-kosher food or anything like that.
I mean--
I mean, it's just impossible, particularly under the circumstances when, like I said, you're discriminated against.
But I want to come back to this.
Let me just come back to what happened, and maybe this will come out anyhow.
So we were sent to a Jewish school, like I mentioned before, in [INAUDIBLE].
And now the time had come-- time had marched on, and I think we're now in 1940.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The war had progressed, the Germans had marched into Poland in the meantime, and there was the bombing going on of England with the rockets and all this.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on this one night, we heard the sirens going, and, of course, there had been drilling-- drilling in the sense of fire drills going on-- and in our building, every building had to have wardens that would look after security with-- they had to be-- you know, all the things that go with war time drill and all that sort of thing.
And we partook in all that.
We were part of it.
Anyway, on this day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sirens started to wail.
We went into the cellars.
And--
Of the building--
About 50.
Children.
Yeah.
We had--
--Half a dozen girls, which were about 16, 17, about two or three years older than us.
No, they were just part of the group, but in sort of-- obviously in separate room.
I don't know what the reason was for those girls to be there.
I mean, if there was any other reason, I can't tell you that.
But all the others, I was amongst the oldest of the children there, and I was in my 13th year.
And I'm repeating that because I'll tell you--
Because that's important for a reason which you'll-- you'll see later.
Anyway, the bombing raid took place and, of course, next day when we went to go to school, what happened was the first bombs that fell on Hamburg destroyed our school.
So it was a bit-- we thought it peculiar to see that the English go to destroy German whatever, and the first thing they should hit is a Jewish school.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, there was no school there.
So that was the end of our going to school.
What was arranged afterwards that we had private teachers come in and we used to go-- and then, of course, the air raids became more frequent and more frequent, and the bombs came closer and closer.

And I mean, I distinctly remember-- and this is some of the stupid things children or young people do, or people in danger do sometimes, and I think I mentioned to you before that in a couple of cases, but this was one of the things that-- I was in charge of-- one of the wardens that would go out the building to see if any incendiary bombs might have fallen on the building.
You know?
And we would stand up on the roof and we would watch these bombs falling on St.
Pauli, which is a harbor area, which wasn't far away, and destroy-- start destroying these buildings and all that, not even giving a thought of it that, hey, the next one might fall on our building.
And we were happy this was happening.
You know what I'm saying?
It doesn't make sense when you think about it logically, but that was the way we were feeling, that it was good that this was taking place and--
Yeah.
Right, that, you know, maybe this--
Well, it was bombed by accident, yeah--
It was bombed because the area.
--But the thing is that this was only one, but, I mean, we lived with that now for months after that.
And during that time, St.
Pauli was completely destroyed, and Hamburg really took a hammering.
And we moved around Hamburg.
We were allowed only certain-- to go into certain areas.
Not only because--
I'm not quite sure.
This is a moot point.
I can't remember.
I really can't remember.
I seem to remember-- not a star, but a-- I seem to remember something and then I don't.
You know?
It's sort of gone out of my mind.
It's just like-- the mind is a funny thing.
After I left this place, I could not remember the address even.
I just wiped it out of my mind and I don't know whether I should go into that now, but maybe--
OK, I'll just talk about it because I might forget, and I thought it was very interesting.
After the war-- no, no I won't go into it.
I'll come back--
Remind me, I'll come back after.
So what happened here was, we kept ourselves busy.
We learned Hebrew.
We had all sorts of other lectures.
And then some of those girls, the seven girls that I was talking about, were taken away.
But one of them, who was a particularly close friend of mine, told me that they're going to try and escape to-- to Romania-- via Romania to try and go on a boat to Israel.
Now these girls then left, and that thing sort of stuck in my mind, that somebody is trying to get out via other countries to get to Palestine.
And while I think of it--
Yes.

And while I'm thinking of it, whilst looking through some of my papers, I found a letter that the girls wrote to me from [INAUDIBLE] in 1941, after they heard that we had arrived, and telling us some of their experiences.
Yes.
They had finally arrived in Palestine, and they came there on a boat which was in-- blown up in Haifa Harbor.
And unfortunately, one of the girls got drowned in that incident.
So she went a long way for nothing.
You know, but--
Yeah.
Anyway, to come back to-- this was the [? patria? ?]

So, to come back to the story, what happened is, when you live in a group of young children, confined-- going on hikes every now and then, but still very confined-- you get to know people, and you get to know some of their worries and some of their things.
And we were talking about families, what happened to this, what happened to that.
And one thing we noticed, and one thing was very important, that in those days, still, birthdays were a-- at that age-- were an important part of your life.
We started to notice a pattern, that whenever somebody's 14th birthday came near, they had to go.
They were going home.
They were going here.
They were going there.
They were going to visit an uncle.
And after a while, I became suspicious, and I talked to this good friend of mine about it, and we were keeping an eye on it.
I was coming up to 14.
Yeah.
And-- so one night, we broke into the office, and we saw-- we found, kept with meticulous--
This was the office of the person-- our leader.
Yeah.
He was in his 40's.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Just one, yes.
I mean, he was acting under orders from I don't know who.
No.
Well, did he or didn't he?
Actually, I'm not sure.
Yeah, it was all organized.
You know, and I really know very little about it.
I know some of the things we did, but sort of never--
--Concerned us or--
No.
Yes, Hardy Plauter.
Hardy Plauter, P-L-A-U-T.
Yes.
I've got his photo here, too.
[LAUGHS]

So anyway, to-- we found out that these children were taken away on the orders of the Gestapo and sent to Sachsenhausen, which we knew to be a concentration camp, to be sent from there to other places and wherever.
I've got no idea.
The point was, what we learned from that was that, hey, they're not going home.
They're not going to visit their uncles and all that sort of thing, that there is a scheme in this whole situation.
So we faced him, and he finally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] owned up.
Yes, there is, but please don't talk about it, because the fact is, as upset he is about it, there's nothing he can do about it.
That was his point of view.
Yeah.
So we talked about it and we said, OK, if we're going to sit here and, you know, we're getting close to 14th-- and it didn't take place exactly on the 14th.
You know, it was around about that time.
Yeah.
I mean, it could be two months later, it could be at the--
So we said, OK.
We decided we're going to run away.
You know, it happens even today that kids run away, so there were-- under those circumstances.
The fact was, I had been through.
I knew what happened to my father, that he had to disappear, and I said, OK.
My time's come.
I've got to make an effort.
And it was either-- what can happen?
If I get caught, I get sent to camp with its consequences of possible-- we didn't know at that time.
We knew there were camps.
We knew that it wasn't a [NON-ENGLISH].
It wasn't a-- a place where you go for [INAUDIBLE].
Holiday camp.
Holiday camp.
But we didn't know the seriousness of the situation, of the eventual--
Well, we thought that they-- we didn't know what they're going to-- what's going to happen, but we knew it wasn't a good place to go.
To be confined and all this whole thing.
So we decided to run away, and we told him so.
And he said, look, I can't do that because I have certain responsibilities.
But if your mother will give you the OK, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then there's nothing I can do about it.
So we made a call to my mother, and-- it was a difficult time.
Well, regular is one-- you know, you can say regular.
I used to write cards to her and I used to get a few cards back.
Telephones in those days were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rare things.
It was expensive, we had no money, but there was contact.
No.
Anyway, I called my mother-- I was lucky to get her-- and I explained the situation to her.
And I said, look-- I knew I had to be careful of what I said.
And I said to her, I wasn't happy here.
I didn't like-- I didn't want to stay and gave all sorts of excuses why, you know, I didn't like it.
So she said, well, what do you want to do?
She didn't really want me to come back home, because she wasn't allowed to leave the place.
And what are you going to do?
And I said, well, I want to visit Uncle Chaim.
Uncle Chaim was her brother.
And there was a silence at the other side for a while, and then she said, OK.
If that's what you want to do, that's fine.
And of course, I must explain here that her brother, Chaim, was living in Tel Aviv.
And she got the message of what I was trying to do.
I mean, she knew that I'm going to give it a go and try and get out of Germany with the eventual--
It was a code you were trying to give her--
It was a code.
Yes.
That you were going to Israel without saying you were going to go.
We're speaking through the [INAUDIBLE].
You know, the--
You knew the phone call could be overheard.
It was a type of language that was used not to-- not to spell it out, because, I mean, I was-- I would have given myself away if I would have said I want to escape.
And she agreed.
He listened in, and he said, OK, if she says that's fine.
So we then started making a few inquiries, and I must say he was helpful with that, and put us onto an address in Berlin that when we were going to go.
And we just packed a little parcel for ourselves.

The date had come, I advised my mother, and by pure coincidence, my late mother had asked for permission to leave Aken to visit Berlin, to fix up something, and it coincided on the day that-- I knew when she was going to be there and I left Hamburg with this friend of mine, and--
Vitus.
V-I-T-U-S.
Mandelbaum.
And we arrived in Berlin, and I met my mother at the station there.
No, sorry.
I met her at my aunt's place.
In Berlin.
I had an aunt living there.
My mother took me to the station when we were leaving.
And that's the last time I saw them, that was-- together with my brother and sister.
Yeah, and she went back to Aken and then, we went on from there.
But I must say here, we met-- we got in touch with an organization in Berlin that was taking care of homeless people-- Jewish people, or people in need-- and I can't remember the title of it.
But we were put in touch with an organization that was attempting and giving guidance to young children to escape, and, of course, helping them to do so.
An organization which was led by a lady [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the name of Recha Freier.
R-E-C-H-A, Recha.
Freier.
I think it was F-R-E-I-E-R.
She was the wife of a Berlin rabbi.
I believe they were separated.
But she had taken it up on herself--
She would have been in her 40s.
To me, at the time, she looked like an old lady.
And of course, it's also the way she dressed.
She always had long dresses on and all this sort of thing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must add here, there was another aspect which was rather important.
Recha had taken it upon herself to, particularly, take children who were of originally Polish background.

Because for some peculiar reasons, whenever there were places-- like, for instance, there were places given to young children transports to go to Switzerland or to Sweden-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the preference was always given to young German Jewish kids.
Meaning Jews coming from the east.
Yeah.
They were, [INAUDIBLE] at the time.
They were looked down, not unlike what happened when the [INAUDIBLE] came to this country by the establishment Jews in Australia.
You know the history a little bit about that.
It's an unfortunate situation, but it's true, nevertheless.
So she had taken it upon herself to particularly look after, or give preference, to those children.
Not exclusively, but anyway.
So we were put in touch with 10 other young children, the youngest of whom was six years old.
His parents had been taken to a concentration camp.
The only person he had left was an uncle in Tel Aviv, and the kid was by himself.
So he was given-- we were taking charge of him.
Where was he from?
I don't-- he was German, but I don't remember where.
So anyway--
Yeah, he told us.
We were told by these people that he had told them and--
The aim was to-- yeah.
The aim was to get him to-- to Palestine.
So here we are in Berlin.
We're given a time.
We're told, now, this and this is what you do, and what you do is you try and make your way to Vienna.
In Vienna, you will be met by somebody who will guide you further.
And then that's what took place.
So we left Berlin.
Oh, only for maybe a week.
Yeah.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't even a week.
It was about four days.
So I-- my mother was at the station.
We said goodbye, and we left there together with an adult lady who accompanied us to Vienna with her daughter.
And I'm not quite sure.
She was sort of a chaperone, if you like.
But we were really told beforehand, yes, she's your chaperone.
Listen to her, you know, if you can.
On the other hand, remember you're on your own.
That was given to all of us.
I mean, when the time comes-- you know, you're going as a group, but each one for himself, type of thing.
You know, try and help each other, but--
Oh yeah, we had-- we had briefings--
--Beforehand of this is what we were going to do.
And this was not-- we were not the only group.
I mean, there were-- you know, there were people-- this was an organization that was going on all the time.
Of course, it was very much improved on later on-- in later war years where people, like Hannah Szenes and other people, you know, parachuted in behind the lines and all that to get people out.
But this was the beginning of it.
And so we proceeded to Vienna, we got there--
Who?
Just a little-- just a little satchel.
You know, a change of clothes.
A couple of shirts and a bit of underwear and, you know, a spare pair of shoes.
And there were some personal things.
I mean, I had my-- my prayer shawl.
I'm saying it in English-- a Tallit.
And tefillin and, you know, some photographs.
And-- and my mother, I remember, gave me some gold coinage that she had.
No, no.
It was some gold coins, which was money, if you like.
But it was just to have something of value in case I might need it.
I don't know what the other kids had in their thing, you know, but it was-- wasn't a big deal.
So we got to Vienna.
We were met by the person.
We then went from Vienna by bus to Graz, which is--
Graz.
[SIGHS] I'm not quite sure now.
Maybe I should have looked it up.
It's about 40 kilometers out towards the-- towards the Alps, but towards the Yugoslav border.
Within Austria.
Yeah.
G-R-A-Z.
If you like, you can look it up after.
So there, again, we were met by-- an organized-- like, and organized into a group, which we had already-- but by a guide.
And this guide, his job was to try and get us across the border-- that is, the Austrian border-- into Yugoslavia.
Now this is December, 1940.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's winter time.
It's cold, and something happened there--
Oh yes.
We had a leather jacket.
We-- something happened.
There were two guides, and I think one of them disappeared.
But anyway, that doesn't matter.
We went with our guide and we started--
Yeah.
So we started walking, and we walked for not only days but weeks, you know, in the Alps.
And this guy used to take us-- and he had connections.
He would find-- talk to farms that used to be up and he used to know huts where we could stay overnight.
And--
Yeah.
This was a question of just getting across the Alps.

We finally got to the German border, and then he had to work out a time, because the border guards were changing at certain times and the thing was to get across the border when the change of guard was taking place.
And then, from the border, you would get in-- we were told and it was so-- we would get into an area which was called a no man's land.
I don't know exactly how deep it was.
And then we had-- the second thing is to get into the Yugoslav, or Croatian, side border.
And they were, of course, on the lookout for people as well, because they didn't want to have these people in their country.
If you were caught, you were just sent back and then you either went to camp, or you-- or you got away and you tried again.
Anyway, we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived under very hard circumstances in the woods.
You know, sometimes with potato peels, whatever the farmers used to throw out, and--
Yeah.
And other times, tree barks and-- whatever it was that we could find.
Oh yes, it was.
It was, because this was December and we didn't arrive till late January in-- on-- to the other side, which was [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, he was with us.
Anyway, as we were crossing the border, the time came to-- to go, because it's impossible to just sit there and wait.
We managed to get across the German border and the German guards woke up that something was moving and suddenly, they started shooting.
Now, this was the middle of the night and-- I can only describe it this way.
We were on a narrow icy path with a sheer cliff wall on the left side, narrow path, and an abyss on the other side.
And we were walking in one line.
And they started shooting.
We started not running but going very fast.
And the narrow path was very covered in ice and that, and a fellow, not immediately in front of me, but two in front of me, slipped.
Well, at least I think he slipped.
It was either slipped or he was shot.
I don't know, but he fell.
The Germans.
He fell.
The fellow behind him tried to get hold of him and got caught up with him, and the two of them just disappeared in front of me.
And I don't know why but we-- I just kept walking.
You know, it was pitch dark.
And so we lost two of the boys, never heard of them again, never knew what actually happened to them.
Well, they were 14.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got across, away from the German border.
Then there was some more shooting, and, you know, anyway, we managed to get across that border.
We managed to get across to the other side, and we were met there by a different guide who then, over a period of days, took us to a place called Maribor.
I mean, I don't want to stay on this whole escapade day by day because--
He took us to a place called Maribor, which was the closest city to-- that we came to.
We were in Croatia at this time.
Now, at this stage, two cars were supposed to meet us, and this was part of the organization from Zagreb, which was probably another 100 kilometers away.
Or maybe not even that.
I can't remember exactly the distances.
We knew our destination was Zagreb.
This is where the Recha Freier was sitting, waiting for us.
When I say sitting, she was-- that was where her organization was.
Right.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so when we arrived in Maribor, at the appointed time, only one car turned up.
Now, you can't put 10 children into one car.
Five had to stay behind.
The promise was that the car would come back the next day and pick us up, or another car.
But it never came.
It turned out-- and the driver told us, if you can find, there's a Jewish family, they are called Deutsch.
Deitz?
Deutsch?
It was a timber merchant.
He will hide you for a day or the night, and that's what happened.
We stayed with that family for a week.
The five of you?
The other five?
The five of us.
And he fed us.
He hid us in his timber yard.
And when the time went on and there was no sight of anybody coming to pick us up, he said, look, I'm in danger myself.
We can't go on like this.
We have to do something.
What are we going to do?
He said, look, I suggest I'm going to buy train tickets for you.
And so he did.
He got five tickets, and-- We'll have a tape change and we'll--
